exemplar.title:
To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County in Kenya,
influenced by wind processes and vegetation cover?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County in Kenya,
influenced by wind processes and vegetation cover?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with explicit links to aeolian and succession theory allowing testable hypotheses.
Precise geographic context at Mambrui with exact coordinates and feasibility for primary data collection.
Locational maps include a legend and north arrow, effectively orienting the reader.
weaknesses: Mapping conventions are partially met: scale bars are missing and map resolution is low.
The theoretical framework section is truncated in places and could more fully articulate links to aeolian theory.
Hypothesis 3 lacks explicit theoretical justification, reducing completeness of theoretical linkage.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands demonstrate clear, theoretically grounded formulation and precise location specificity (both at band 3), while mapping falls just short of full conventions (band 2). Holistically, the work meets the highest level for question and context and deserves 3 marks, with minor deductions for mapping conventions and completeness of theory.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are clearly described and explicitly linked to the research question.
Systematic sampling with three trials per variable demonstrates an awareness of reliability.
Use of digital anemometer and quadrat reflects appropriate primary data collection techniques.
weaknesses: Some improvised techniques (balloon wind vane, tennisâball drift) introduce potential bias and lack standardization.
Notation symbols are not explained, reducing clarity for the reader.
Justification of tool calibration (e.g., clinometer accuracy) is incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands (method relevance/description and data collection implementation) align with the middle band (band 2). While methods are appropriate and clearly linked, they lack comprehensive explanation and introduce some reliability concerns, warranting 2 marks.

strengths: A broad and relevant dataset across four variables and 16 sites provides sufficient evidence for analysis.
Presentation uses a variety of appropriate graphs and tables with clear labeling.
Figures generally adhere to conventions with titles and legends.
weaknesses: Several rawâdata table cells are blank and units are inconsistently applied.
Some graphs lack axis units and error bars, reducing interpretive reliability.
Occasional lapses in notation (Greek symbols) indicate inconsistent convention adherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and sufficiency meets the upper 3â4 band (band 4), presentation techniques are solid but not exceptional (band 3), and conventions show only occasional errors (band 3). Holistically this aligns with the 5â6-mark rangeâs lower half; a score of 4 reflects generally effective treatment with room for stronger presentation and full convention adherence.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and statistical techniques, including Spearmanâs rank with confidence levels.
Data interpretation identifies key trends and anomalies and links them to Aeolian and succession theory.
All hypotheses are addressed with detailed discussion, demonstrating a high level of analytical engagement.
weaknesses: Error analysis and deeper quantitative application (e.g., Bernoulli equation, RÂ² values) are limited.
Some sections remain descriptive rather than fully quantitative, leaving minor analytical gaps.
Intermediate calculation steps could be formatted more clearly for readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work moves beyond basic description with correct statistical tests and confidence levels (band 5â6). Trend interpretations are linked to theory, and most gaps are minor. Given the strong but not fully comprehensive statistical treatment, 6 marks reflect the upper end of the 5â6 band.

strengths: Conclusion clearly revisits each hypothesis and is fully supported by the analysis.
Connections between findings and research aims are explicit and coherent.
weaknesses: Could further reflect on broader geographic implications or suggest future research angles within the conclusion itself.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets all requirements for a fully supported, coherent summary of findings, placing it firmly in the top band for Criterion E and meriting 2 marks.

strengths: Identifies both strengths (use of digital equipment) and weaknesses (balloon method, time constraints) with clear relevance.
Improvement suggestions (more study points, better equipment) are appropriate to the study design.
weaknesses: Suggestions lack detailed explanation of how each change would specifically enhance validity or reliability.
Opportunities to expand on methodological improvements (e.g., landâuse mapping) are noted but not fully developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths, weaknesses, and practical improvements (band 2). While appropriate, it does not offer the comprehensive, impact-focused discussion required for the top band, so 2 marks are awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two locational maps are provided, with a key and north arrow; however, scale bars are absent and resolution is low, so mapping conventions are only partially met, aligning with the middle band.

The research question is explicitly geographical, clearly focused on aeolian processes and vegetation in a precise coastal setting, and it lends itself to measurable hypotheses.  Three testable hypotheses are stated and linked to recognized coastal and succession theory, fulfilling the highest descriptor.

Tables and a range of graphs (line, bar, scatter) are appropriate and clearly labelled, but some graphs lack axis units or error bars, and more advanced presentation (e.g., overlay plots, GIS analysis) is absent.  This fits solid but not exceptional presentation.

Digital anemometer and quadrat use show correct technique, but improvised wind-vane and tennis-ball longshore drift introduce reliability issues.  Data volume (16 sites, 3 trials) is decent but quality is mixed, suiting the middle band.

Descriptive statistics, Spearmanâs Rank and trend lines are correctly applied, demonstrating appropriate statistical techniques beyond simple description, but confidence levels and error analysis are limited, so the work meets but does not exceed the 3-4 band.

Trends and anomalies for each variable are discussed and linked to Aeolian and succession theory, yet links are sometimes general (e.g., Bernoulli principle citation without calculation).  Interpretation is solid but not exhaustive.

The student specifies Mambruiâs exact coordinates, justifies accessibility, and explains feasibility of primary data at 16 systematically spaced sites.  This precision clearly supports primary fieldwork.
The student identifies strengths (digital equipment) and weaknesses (balloon method, time limits) with brief explanation of their impact, giving an appropriate but not comprehensive evaluation.

Four primary variables measured across 16 points supply generally sufficient, relevant data for the stated hypotheses, though a few missing vegetation values reduce completeness.  Overall, evidence supports the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion revisits each hypothesis and cites supporting evidence from graphs and statistics, providing clear, consistent linkage to findings; it fully supports the research aims, satisfying the top band for Criterion E.

All three hypotheses are addressed, but analysis of longshore drift and wind direction remains descriptive and some data gaps (missing early vegetation values) are unexplained, leaving moderate omissions.

Methods for each variable are clearly described and explicitly related to the research question, yet explanations are uneven (e.g., improvised balloon for wind direction, limited justification of clinometer accuracy).  Detail is sufficient but not fully comprehensive.

Most figures have titles and legends, yet inconsistencies in unit notation (km/h vs âwind speedâ) and the use of Greek symbols in tables indicate occasional lapses, consistent with âoccasional errorsâ.
Suggestionsâuse more study points, improve equipmentâare relevant but lack detailed discussion of how each change would enhance validity or reliability, aligning with the middle band.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive, aiding navigation, but including figure and table numbers beside each entry would improve clarity.
Figure 1 provides a clear locational map with legend and north arrow, but adding a scale bar would fully meet mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2âs ArcGIS map resolution is low; improve image quality and include a scale bar to strengthen spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context paragraph includes rich cultural detail but strays from geographic relevance; focus on physical characteristics and coordinates.
Variables are listed clearly, but the use of symbols like \overline{} and â  is not explained. Include a key for symbol notation.
The research question is clearly geographical, focused on aeolian processes and vegetation cover in a precise coastal setting, and aligns well with theory to allow testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The theory link section appears truncated, leaving the theoretical framework incomplete. Ensure full descriptions of Aeolian processes are provided.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The quadrat and ACFOR method are appropriate but converting qualitative scores to quantitative data could introduce subjectivity; describe mitigation strategies.
The ACFOR scale table is well presented. Consider adding error ranges or confidence intervals when mapping qualitative to quantitative values.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 3 is stated but lacks theoretical justification. Link it explicitly to aeolian theory or succession processes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The systematic sampling rationale is strong and well justified for time constraints, demonstrating clear relevance to the research design.
Listing all variables with three trials shows a commitment to reliability. Consider commenting on calibration of tools to further bolster accuracy.
The anemometer procedure is clear, but switching off the device could disrupt response time; specify how this practice affects measurement consistency.
The Spearmanâs Rank explanation is thorough and includes confidence level, demonstrating strong statistical application beyond basic description.
Conclusion succinctly summarizes each hypothesis and refers explicitly to supporting data, demonstrating strong alignment with the research aims.
The improvised tennis ball technique for longshore drift introduces potential bias; consider a standardized sediment trap or dye method.
Raw data table has several empty cells; clarify whether data were not collected or omitted, and include units consistently for readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 6âs caption is clear but the graph itself lacks axis units and error bars, reducing interpretation reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The discussion invokes Bernoulliâs principle without quantitative application. Including the equation $$v=\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$$ could strengthen the link.
Vegetation cover analysis effectively links to succession theory and shows clear trend; consider adding statistical test (e.g., linear regression RÂ² value).
The calculation steps in the equation block are clear; separate intermediate results (e.g., $$\sum d^2$$) into distinct lines for readability.
The critical values table (Table 4) is well sourced; however, highlight the chosen significance level (e.g. 0.05) to guide interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The extension proposing anthropogenic impacts is insightful; specify potential methods (e.g., landâuse mapping or interviews) to develop this idea.
References are plentiful but inconsistent in format; apply a consistent citation style (e.g., APA) across all entries.
